info.topictitle: 4.6.2 Components of the balance of payments

markdown:
<definition term="Balance of Payments">A **statement** or **record** of a **country's** **economic** **transactions** with the all other **countries** over a specific **time** **period** (often a year).</definition>

The **balance of payments** has three components:

1. **Current account**
2. **Capital account**
3. **Financial account**

### Calculation: Elements of Balance of Payments


This table **below** will be **utilised** throughout the explanation of all components.

| Line | Components | Figures (Millions of dollars) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Current Account |  |
| 2 | Exports of Goods | +25 |
| 3 | Imports of Goods | -50 |
| 4 | Balance of Trade in Goods (Line 2 - 3) | -25 |
| 5 | Exports of Services | +15 |
| 6 | Imports of Services | -5 |
| 7 | Balance of Trade in Services (Line 5 - 6) | +10 |
| 8 | Balance of Trade in Goods & Services (Line 4 + 7) | -15 |
| 9 | Income (inflows - outflows) | -4 |
| 10 | Current Transfers (inflows - outflows) | +1 |
| 11 | Balance on Current Account (Line 8 + 9 + 10) | -18 |
|  |  |  |
| 12 | Capital Account |  |
| 13 | Capital Transfers (inflows - outflows) | +1.1 |
| 14 | Transactions non-financial assets (inflows - outflows) | +0.9 |
| 15 | Balance on Capital Account (Line 13 + 14) | +2 |
|  |  |  |
| 16 | Financial Account |  |
| 17 | Foreign Direct Investment (inflows - outflows) (FDI; inflows - outflows) | +18 |
| 18 | Portfolio Investment (inflows - outflows) | -3 |
| 19 | Reserve Assets (official reserves) | +2 |
| 20 | Official Borrowing | -1 |
| 21 | Balance of Financial Account (Line 17 + 18 + 19 + 20) | +16 |
|  |  |  |
| 22 | Balance (Line 11 + 15 + 21) | 0 |

### Current Account


<definition term="Current Account">The **current account** is the **sum** of the **balance of trade in goods and services**, **income** and **current transfers**.</definition>

#### Balance of trade in goods


1. Exports are the goods produced domestically in **Country A** and sold to foreign countries.
  1. The payment for these exports are received in the exporting country's currency.
  2. Hence, **exports** are considered to be **credit** as it is the **inflow** of money.
2. Meanwhile, imports are the goods produced in foreign countries which are purchased by **Country A**.
  1. The payment for these imports are made in the country's currency **Country A **imports from (increasing supply of **Country A's **currency).
  2. Hence, **imports** are considered to be **debit** as it is the **outflow** of money.
3. Thereby, the balance of trade in goods can be calculated by:
  1. $\text{Balance of Trade in Goods} = \text{Export of Goods} - \text{Import of Goods}$

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table:

Country A's **balance of trade in goods** is -25 million dollars.

This is **because**:

$\text{Balance of Trade in goods} = +25 - 50 = -25 \text{ million dollars}$</callout>

#### Balance of trade in services


The **same** **applies** for **services**.

1. Exports are the services created domestically in **Country A** and sold to foreigners.
  1. Services include **tourism** for **foreign individuals**, **consulting**, **transportation**, **insurance**, etc.
  2. When foreigners buy the following services, it is **considered** as the **export** of **services**.
  3. Hence, **exports** are considered to be **credit** as it is the **inflow** of money.
2. Meanwhile, imports are the services created in foreign countries which are purchased by **Country A **citizens.
  1. When **Country A** citizens **purchase** tourism packages or insurance from **foreign** **firms**, it is considered as the **import** of **services**. 
  2. Hence, **imports** are considered to be **debit** as it is the **outflow** of money.
3. Thereby, the balance of trade in services can be **calculated** by:
  1. $\text{Balance of Trade in Services} = \text{Export of Services} - \text{Imports of Services}$

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table:

Country A's **balance of trade in services** is +10 million dollars.

This is **because**:

$\text{Balance of Trade in services} = +15 - 5 = +10 \text{ million dollars}$</callout>

#### Balance of trade in goods & services


**Often** referred to as "_Balance of Trade_", it can be calculated by:

$\text{Balance of Trade} = \text{Balance of Trade in Goods} + \text{Balance of Trade in Services}$

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table:

Country A's **balance of trade in goods & services** is -15 million dollars.

This is **because**:

$\text{Balance of Trade} = -25 + 10 = -15 \text{ million dollars}$</callout>

#### Income


**Income** is considered to be all the **inflows** and **outflows** of **factor** **incomes**: **wages, interest, rent, **and **profits**.

1. **Credits** are **inflows** from **foreign** **investments**.
  1. This could also refer to **Country A**'s citizens who might **earn** **income** from abroad such as **having** bank accounts which **earn** **interest**.
2. **Debits** are **outflows** to **foreign** **investors**.
  1. This could refer to **Country A **citizens who might need to **pay** **rent** for housing abroad. 
3. Therefore, **income** is:
  1. $\text{Income} = \text{Inflow of Income} - \text{Outflow of Income}$

<callout type="example">Observing the **table**, the **inflow - outflow** **calculations** had been **provided**.

Therefore, Country A's **income** is -4 million dollars.</callout>

#### Current transfers


**Current transfers** are **inflows** and **outflows** of **funds** for which no goods or services are exchanged.

1. **Credits** are **inflows** from **foreign** **aid, transfers** from **abroad** such as **remittances **(money sent to close family in **Country A **from abroad) or **gifts**, and **pensions**. 
2. **Debits** are **outflows** for **aid** or **remittances** sent **abroad.**
3. $\text{Current Transfers}$ $=$  $\text{Inflow into Country A}$  $-$ $\text{Outflow out of Country A}$

<callout type="example">Observing the **table**, the** inflow - outflow calculations** had been **provided**.

Therefore, Country A's** current transfers **is +1 million dollars.</callout>

#### Balance on Current Account


1. Summing up the components mentioned above provides us the **current account balance.**
2. If there is a larger quantity of credits than debits, then **Country A** would have **current account surplus**.
3. If there is a larger quantity of debits than credits, then **Country A** would have **current account deficit**.

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table, the** balance on current accoun**t is **calculated** from **summing up** all the **three** sub-components.

Therefore, Country A's** balance on current account** is -18 million dollars as:

$\text{Balance on Current Account} = -15 -4 + 1= -18 \text{ million dollars}$</callout>

### Capital Account


<definition term="Capital Account">The **capital account** is the **sum** of the **balance of capital transfers **and **transactions **in **non-produced, non financial assets**.</definition>

<callout type="note">The **current account** and **financial accounts** are **relatively** **larger** than the **capital account **when comparing.</callout>

#### Capital Transfers


**Capital transfers** is the (**inflows -** **outflows** of) **funds** exchanged when an **asset changes** **ownership** from one party to another.

1. **Examples** of capital transfers include: **debt forgiveness, investment grants **(provided by governments)**, sale of fixed assets**, etc.
2. If there is a** large outflow of capital transfers than inflow**, then **Country A** will have **negative** (-) capital account transfers.
3. If there is a** large inflow of capital transfers than outflow**, then **Country A** will have **positive** (+) capital account transfers.
4. This can be calculated by: $\text{Capital Transfers} = \text{Inflow of transfers} - \text{Outflow of transfers}$

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table, the **inflow - outflow **calculations had been **provided**.

Therefore, Country A's** capital transfers **is +1.1 million dollars.</callout>

#### Transactions in non-produced, non-financial assets


1. Transactions in this **category mainly** are **composed** of **purchases** or use of **natural** **resources** which **cannot/are not produced.**
2. This includes: l**and, mineral rights, forestry rights, airspace, electromagnetic spectrum, etc**.
3. As with other components, if there is a** large outflow of funds than inflow for such transactions**, then **Country A** will have **negative** (-) in transactions in non-produced, non-financial assets.
4. If there is a** large inflow of funds than outflow for such transactions**, then **Country A** will have **positive** (+) in transactions in non-produced, non-financial assets.
5. This can be calculated by: $\text{Transactions in non-produced, non-financial assets} = \text{Inflow} - \text{Outflow}$

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table, the **inflow - outflow** calculations had been **provided**.

Therefore, Country A's **transactions non-financial assets** is +0.9 million dollars.</callout>

#### Balance on Capital Account


1. The sum of the two components mentioned above provides the **balance of capital account**.
2. If there is a larger quantity of credits than debits, then **Country A** would have **capital account surplus**.
3. If there is a larger quantity of debits than credits, then **Country A** would have **capital account deficit**.

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table:

The** balance on capital accoun**t is calculated from **summing up** the **two** sub-components.

Therefore, Country A's **capital account **is +2 million dollars as:

$\text{Balance on Capital Account} = +1.1 + 0.9= +18 \text{ million dollars}$</callout>

### Financial Account


<definition term="Financial Account">The **financial account** is the **sum** of** foreign direct investment (FDI)**, **portfolio investment, reserve assets** and **official borrowing**.</definition>

#### Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)


1. Foreign Direct Investment _(discussed in detail in 4.10) _are investments in **physical** **capital** such as** buildings or factories**.
2. As with** other components**, the **final** **FDI value** is **calculated** by the **quantity** of **credits** and **debits**, which is given in equation below.

$\text{FDI} = \text{foreign investment in Country A} - \text{Country A investment abroad}$

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table, the** inflow - outflow** calculations had been **provided**.

Therefore, Country A's **foreign direct investment** is +18 million dollars.</callout>

#### Portfolio Investment


1. Portfolio Investment represents the investments in **financial capital** such as **bonds and stocks **in the stock market. 
2. This can be calculated by: $\text{FDI} = \text{Foreign investment} - \text{Investment by Country A citizens abroad}$

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table, the** inflow - outflow **calculations had been **provided**.

Therefore, Country A's **portfolio investment** is -3 million dollars.</callout>

#### Reserve Assets


1. Also known as **official reserves**, it **represents the foreign currency reserves **that the central bank can** buy or sell **to adjust the country's currency value.
2. **Outflow** of the currency will **indicate** a **debit** in the **financial** account.
3. **Inflow** of the currency will **indicate** a **credit** in the **financial** account.

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table, the** inflow - outflow** calculations had been **provided**.

Therefore, Country A's **reserve assets **is +2 million dollars.</callout>

#### Official Borrowing


1. This **represents** the **borrowing governments** might **have** from abroad.
2. **Inflow of funds **represent as **borrowing** by the government of **Country A** and **appear** as **credits**.
3. **Outflow of funds** represent **provision of loans **from **Country A **to foreign countries and **appear** as **debits**.

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table, the **inflow - outflow **calculations had been **provided**.

Therefore, Country A's **official borrowing **is -1 million dollars.</callout>

#### Balance on Financial Account


1. The sum of the four components mentioned above provides the **balance of financial account**.
2. If there is a larger quantity of credits than debits, then **Country A** would have **financial account surplus**.
3. If there is a larger quantity of debits than credits, then **Country A** would have **financial account deficit**.

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table:

**Summing up the 4 sub-components,** Country A's **balance on financial account** is +16 million dollars.

This is **because**:

$\text{Balance of Trade in services} = + 18 - 3 + 2 - 1 = +16 \text{ million dollars}$</callout>

### Clarifications


#### Errors and Omissions


1. It is quite **difficult** and is **considered** **impossible** to **record** every possible transaction that a **country** **does** with all other countries. Due to this, some **transactions go unrecorded**.
2. Though, as the** surpluses must match the deficits**, there **needs** to be an component which creates the **equality**.
3. Therefore, there is a component known as **errors and omissions** which create the equality between the** surpluses and deficits**.

<callout type="note">This can appear in **certain** balance of statements! However for the **purpose** of **learning**, this has been **voided** in the **example** **provided**.</callout>

#### Capital Account and Financial Account


1. Many economists use the term** _capital account_**_ _to refer to both _capital account and **financial account**._
2. Therefore, if the expression '**capital** **account**' is utilised, it could refer to **financial account and capital account.**

### Balance


1. Summing up **all the balances of all the different accounts **(with errors and omissions if present in the balance of payments table) will **provide the final calculation**. 
2. $\text{Current Account} + \text{Capital Account} + \text{Financial Account} = \text{Balance}
3. The final calculation for **balance of payments will always be zero**.

<callout type="example">**Observing** the table:

This **can be seen** as Country A's **balance** 0 million dollars.

This is **because** by **summing up the three balances**:

$\text{Balance} = -18 + 2 + 16 = 0 \text{ million dollars}$</callout>

<callout type="note">The reason why the** balance of payments** will always** sum up to zero** will be **covered** in _4.6.3 Interdependence between the accounts_.</callout>



<callout type="self_review">1. Explain the **difference** between the** current account **and the** financial account**.

2. How do **capital** **transfers** **differ** from **current transfers**?</callout>



<callout type="tok">How do** balance of payments** **imbalances** reflect **global economic inequalities**?</callout>
